column127: I own a surf school and a sister non-profit focused on connected inner city high-need communities (youth and adults, ages 5-50s/60s) to the ocean. I provide transportation (charter bus) and resources upon arrival (bathing suits, surf gear and equipment, food, temporary shelter, and ocean safety/surf instruction). Our partner recreation centers bring from 45-200 students per visit for 8-9 weeks of summer and select follow-up visits monthly.  The rides to/from the beach can take from 30 minutes to 2 hours depending on traffic congestion and location in LA County. MANY to most of the children we serve can not swim and we are teaching them basics in the ocean to stay safe in open water environments.

column127: I live by the beach and work as a surf instructor so I am there almost every day for work or recreational use.  I am regularly saddened and frustrated by the amount of trash, pollution and lack of care people have towards our beaches.  A lot of it comes from the increased unhoused encampments but thats not the only cause of course… Parking rates just went way up for everyone- i hope at least a portion of that money goes back into beach preservation.. I see some efforts to restore dunes and reduce the impact on the ocean but the amount of “accidental sewage leaks” and “oil spills” and dumping in the ocean is far too much for one person (or one community) to combat single handedly with cleanups etc.  That said, I am proud to live in a city and state that puts conservation and environmental issues on the map.. I am dedicated to doing my part to help keep our oceans and aquatic life healthy and sustainable. It would be absolutely devastating to lose access to the oceans and the peace and beauty of our beaches.
